statement: Guðlaugur Þór Þórðarson (Sjálfstæðisflokkurinn, former Foreign Minister) presented a bill on 29 November 2023 regarding Iceland's participation in the EU Emissions Trading System (ETS). As a flutningsræða of 1,678 words, the speech addressed the mechanics of how ETS obligations are transposed through the EEA Agreement and the implications for Icelandic industry. This parliamentary debate provides primary-source context for claims about Iceland's environmental policy obligations under EEA/EU frameworks and the economic costs of emissions trading compliance.
source_name: Alþingi — Guðlaugur Þór Þórðarson, þingmaður Sjálfstæðisflokksins (flutningsræða, 154. löggjafarþing)
caveats: Speech summary based on metadata — the full policy positions stated in the speech should be verified against the complete text. Guðlaugur Þór served as Foreign Minister under Katrín Jakobsdóttir's government (2017–2021) and his positions may reflect both his ministerial experience and his party's current stance.

related_entries.statement: Iceland generates virtually 100% of its electricity from renewable sources: approximately 70% from hydropower and 30% from geothermal energy. In 2023, total electricity generation was approximately 19.5 TWh, of which roughly 13.5 TWh came from hydropower and 6 TWh from geothermal. This makes Iceland unique among European countries — the EU average share of renewables in electricity generation was approximately 44% in 2023. Only Norway (98% hydro) comes close to Iceland's renewable share. Iceland's per capita electricity consumption is among the highest in the world at roughly 53 MWh per person per year, driven largely by aluminium smelting.

The EU has adopted a vast body of legislation since its founding. As of end 2024, the EUR-Lex database records approximately 146,700 regulations and 4,600 directives adopted since 1958, totalling over 151,000 binding legal acts. During the EEA period (1994–2024), the EU adopted roughly 57,100 regulations (averaging 1,842 per year) and 2,626 directives (85 per year). Legislative output has declined from a peak of approximately 4,000+ regulations per year in the late 1980s to around 1,200 per year in 2019–2023.
